title: 'Regresarías a la CDMX' — Sep 2019 Spanish-language interview prompts
html:
TL;DR

  • Four-line interview-prompt note from Sep 4, 2019, written entirely in Spanish (with one autocorrected "Pero qué fue la mejor decisión que tu has hecho" mangled by Apple Notes into "Perl queen era la me joe decision que tu has hecho"). Yahir is preparing to interview someone — likely a Mexico City–based relative or family friend — using a four-question template.
  • Title is the central question: "Regresarías a la CDMX" — "would you return to [Mexico] City?" The other three: "Did you have an idol growing up," "Que cambiarías de tu vida" (what would you change about your life), and "Would you live here" (in English — the only English line, telling about who he was framing the question for).
  • Earliest extant specimen of Yahir's interview-people-you-want-to-learn-from template applied in Spanish, suggesting the interviewee was a Spanish-dominant elder. The same template later shows up in English form for "Questions for Raissa" in 2019 12 12 talk to brenda (Dec 2019, three months later) — meaning the interview-instinct was bilingual from the start, and the language-of-prompt was selected per-interviewee.
  • The CDMX/return question is identity-loaded — Yahir is a US-born Mexican-American kid in Grants Pass, asking someone older whether they'd reverse the migration. The mirror-image question is "would you live here" — the same question from the other direction. Together they're the migration-decision audit template he later applies to himself with the China move and the Austin move.
  • Sits chronologically right between the Aug 31 "back from Mexico" note and the Sep 10 Phoenix→Portland flight letter. Three notes in 11 days, all about places-and-belonging. He'd just been to Mexico, was preparing for a year-end run, and was thinking through who-stays-where in his lineage.

Key quotes

"Regresarias a la CDMX"

"Did you have an idol growing up"

"Que cambiaries de tu vida"

"Would you live here"

Why it matters in the graph

  • Confirms the interview-as-research-method habit was bilingual and present at 16. Pair against the 2024 Jensen Huang prep questions and the 2026 rankett meetup-prep guides — same template, scaled across nine years and across language registers.
  • The CDMX-return question is the early form of the mom house by the river motivation: family migration math is always alive in the corpus, and this 2019 note is one of its earliest specimens.
  • The bilingual mode itself is significant — the 2024-2025 notes are heavily Spanglish, and this 2019 specimen shows the code-switching was deliberate (per-interviewee, not chaotic) from teen years.

Open questions

  • Who was the interviewee? CDMX-based aunt, uncle, abuela? Worth checking if Yahir has ever named them in later notes.
  • Did the interview ever happen, and were the answers recorded?
  • Does "would you live here" read as here = Grants Pass or here = US? Both are possible at age 16.

Privacy redaction

  • None applied — no identifying info beyond the city of residence (CDMX) and a generic interview frame. The presumed interviewee is not named in the note.

Wikilinks
